questions.q: Which statement best defines a photon as taught in this episode?
options: A continuous stream of electromagnetic energy whose strength depends on brightness
A discrete, indivisible packet (quantum) of light energy whose energy equals Planck's constant times the frequency
A charged particle of light that carries both energy and electric charge
A standing wave of light trapped between two surfaces
questions.explanation: A photon is a discrete, indivisible quantum of electromagnetic energy with \(E = hf\). It is not continuous (the whole quantum idea is that light comes in lumps), and photons are uncharged, ruling out the other options.

questions.q: Using \(E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}\), how does a photon's energy depend on its wavelength?
options: Energy is directly proportional to wavelength
Energy is inversely proportional to wavelength: shorter wavelength means higher energy
Energy is independent of wavelength and set only by intensity
Energy is proportional to the square of the wavelength
questions.explanation: Since \(E = hc/\lambda\), energy is inversely proportional to wavelength — 'short wave, big punch'. A short wavelength is a high-frequency, high-energy photon; a long wavelength is a low-energy photon.

questions.q: A red laser is made brighter while its colour stays the same. What actually changes?
options: Each red photon now carries more energy
The frequency of the red light increases
More red photons are emitted per second, each carrying the same energy as before
The wavelength of the red light shortens
questions.explanation: Brightness (intensity) is set by the number of photons per second, not the energy per photon. Each red photon's energy is fixed by red's frequency, so a brighter red beam means more photons of the same energy — the trap of 'brighter means more energetic photons' is exactly what the episode warns against.

questions.q: Who introduced the relation \(E = hf\) and what did he initially believe about it?
options: Einstein in 1905, and he immediately believed the photon was physically real
Planck in 1900, but he regarded the energy quantum as merely a mathematical trick
Newton in 1704, as part of his particle model of light
Hertz in 1887, after discovering the photoelectric effect
questions.explanation: Max Planck introduced \(E = hf\) in 1900 to fix black-body radiation and thought it just a bookkeeping trick. Einstein, in 1905, gave the photon physical reality. The HSC tests this order, so don't credit Einstein with the equation.

questions.q: Calculate the energy of a photon of wavelength \(6.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m}\). (Use \(h = 6.63\times10^{-34}\,\mathrm{J\,s}\), \(c = 3.0\times10^{8}\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\).)
questions.explanation: \(E = hc/\lambda = (6.63\times10^{-34})(3.0\times10^{8})/(6.0\times10^{-7}) = 3.3\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\). The \(10^{-25}\) distractor forgets to divide by the wavelength.

questions.q: Convert a photon energy of \(3.2 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\) to electron-volts (\(1\,\mathrm{eV} = 1.6\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\)).
options: 0.5 eV
2.0 eV
5.1 eV
3.2 eV
questions.explanation: To convert joules to eV, divide by \(1.6\times10^{-19}\): \(3.2\times10^{-19} / 1.6\times10^{-19} = 2.0\,\mathrm{eV}\).

questions.q: A pulse of light delivers a total energy of \(4.0 \times 10^{-18}\,\mathrm{J}\). If each photon carries \(4.0 \times 10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\), how many photons are in the pulse?
options: 100 photons
10 photons
1 photon
40 photons
questions.explanation: The number of photons is total energy divided by energy per photon: \(4.0\times10^{-18} / 4.0\times10^{-19} = 10\) photons. The units cancel to a pure number, as a photon count must.

questions.q: Why can a brief flicker of ultraviolet light eject electrons from a metal while intense red light cannot, no matter how bright?
options: UV light carries more total energy than red light always
Each UV photon has a higher frequency and therefore enough energy in a single hit, whereas each red photon is too feeble regardless of how many arrive
Red photons are absorbed before reaching the metal surface
Red light travels slower than UV light, so it cannot reach the electrons
questions.explanation: What matters is the energy of each individual photon, set by frequency. A UV photon's high frequency gives it enough energy in one hit; a million feeble red photons are still a million feeble taps. This is the energy-versus-number distinction made visible.

questions.q: Two laser beams have equal total power but different colours: one red (longer \(\lambda\)), one blue (shorter \(\lambda\)). Which contains more photons per second, and why?
options: The blue beam, because higher-energy photons arrive faster
The red beam, because each red photon carries less energy, so more are needed per second to deliver the same power
They contain exactly the same number, since the power is equal
The blue beam, because shorter wavelengths always mean more photons
questions.explanation: Since \(P = N \times E_{\text{photon}}\) and each red photon carries less energy, the red beam needs more photons per second to match the blue beam's power: \(N \propto \lambda\), so equal power and redder light means more photons per second.

questions.q: What does it mean to say light energy is 'quantised'?
options: Energy can be exchanged in any continuous amount down to zero
Energy is exchanged only in whole-number multiples of one photon's worth (\(hf\)) — a photon is all-or-nothing
Energy depends on the square of the amplitude of the light wave
Energy can be split into fractions of a photon when the light is dim
questions.explanation: Quantisation means energy is delivered only in whole lumps of \(hf\); a photon hands over its full energy or nothing at all. There is no such thing as half a photon's worth of energy being exchanged.
